artifacts.content:
Subject: Why I’m really changing tracks

Hey Hanne,

Pixel is snoring on my lecture notes again, so I’m taking that as a sign to write you instead of pretending to read another review on drought-response transcription factors.

I wanted to explain a bit more calmly why I’m pushing so hard to shift properly into the Biochemistry & Biotechnology PhD. It’s not just a “plants are smaller in tubes than in the Ardennes” thing. Since papa’s minor stroke in early March 2025 and all those rehab sessions twice a week at AZ Sint-Jan in Bruges, it’s become obvious I can’t disappear to the Ardennes field sites for three–four days at a time anymore. Being able to hop on the train from Ghent, go with him to the neurology follow-up appointments, and help mama with groceries and paperwork feels more important than counting every moss species on some windy hillside.

The nice part is that the project I’m actually doing in the Molecular Plant Physiology and Biotechnology lab in Ghent already fits the biochem programme: cloning, protein purification, enzyme kinetics, metabolomics. My days are long but at least they’re mostly in one building instead of three provinces.

Also, between February and June I racked up over €1,200 in extra train tickets and cheap B&Bs for those multi-day field campaigns. Reimbursements are glacial, so I ended up paying rent late in May and June and had to arrange a temporary payment plan with the landlord for Sint-Amandsbergstraat 42, bus 3. Not exactly sustainable for the next 3–4 years.

Anyway, how are your little monsters at school? Still refusing to do their huiswerk? I might come this Sunday if papa’s energy is okay – can we do coffee near AZ before his physio?

Kus,
Lena

Subject: Therapy update + PhD chaos

Hey Tom,

I’m home, showered, and no longer smelling like LB medium, so time for the promised therapy debrief.

My huisarts’ referral to UZ Gent back in November 2024 was honestly one of the better things that happened this year. The CBT sessions since December have made it really clear that the constant knot in my stomach and the insomnia aren’t just “normal PhD stress.” A big chunk of it is this weird in-between situation: officially I’m in the Biology PhD that I enrolled in October 2024, but day to day I’m doing pure biochemistry in the lab. My psychologist keeps coming back to this feeling of being trapped in a trajectory that doesn’t match what I actually do or care about.

We talked again today about the programme switch. She agrees that formally moving into the Doctor of Philosophy in Biochemistry and Biotechnology is a key step in reducing the burnout risk and stabilising my sleep and concentration. Apparently my brain likes coherence.

On top of that, you know how ridiculous the coursework is in the current programme. If I stay there, I still have to tick off a bunch of ecology-heavy mandatory courses that I’ll never use. The biochem doctorate has a lighter, more relevant package, which would make it way more realistic to finish on time without drowning in extra ECTS.

Anyway, enough about my neuroses. How was your sprint review? Did the new cloud pipeline behave or did it set itself on fire again? I can cook tomorrow if you want – maybe something that isn’t pasta for once.

See you later (don’t forget cat treats, Pixel is judging you),
Lena

Title: Pros/cons – staying in Biology vs. switching to Biochem

Pros of switching to Doctor of Philosophy in Biochemistry & Biotechnology:
- Actual project fit: what I do all day (molecular cloning, protein expression/purification, enzyme kinetics, metabolomics on Arabidopsis and barley) clearly belongs here, not in a field ecology track.
- Supervision: daily supervision is already from Prof. Dr. Tom Janssens (Biochemistry and Microbiology) and I’m basically embedded in his group and the Molecular Plant Physiology and Biotechnology lab.
- Mental health: since the huisarts sent me to CBT at UZ Gent (first session Dec 2024), it’s obvious that the mismatch between the official Biology enrolment (since Oct 2024) and my biochemical work is fuelling the anxiety + insomnia. Therapist keeps stressing that aligning structure with reality is part of the treatment plan.
- Family logistics: after papa’s minor stroke in early March 2025 and the start of rehab 2x/week at AZ Sint-Jan Bruges, I need work that keeps me mostly in Ghent instead of multi-day Ardennes field campaigns.
- Money: Feb–Jun 2025 = ~€1,200 extra trains + accommodation for fieldwork → late rent in May & June at Sint-Amandsbergstraat 42, bus 3 → awkward temporary payment plan with landlord. Lab-based project uses Tom’s budget, no more fronting costs.

Cons / doubts:
- Feels like admitting I chose the wrong programme when I first signed up for the Biology PhD in Oct 2024 – only realised after a few months that the proposal and the actual doctoral track didn’t really line up.
- Need to navigate politics with the original ecology committee.

Action: talk to Ann & Tom again before next advisory meeting.

metadata.title: Pros/cons – staying in Biology vs. switching to Biochem

artifacts.content:
Subject: Rough committee meeting + question about direction

Dear Ann,

I’ve been meaning to write since the advisory committee meeting on 21 October 2024 in the Department of Biology meeting room. I left that session feeling quite shaken. When one committee member warned that prioritising the molecular work over the planned field ecology could be seen as “deviating from the approved doctoral plan,” and even mentioned the possibility of a formal written warning if my publication strategy stayed focused on biochemical journals, I realised how misaligned my project has become with the expectations of the Biology programme.

At the same time, my day-to-day reality since I enrolled in the Doctor of Philosophy in Biology in October 2024 has drifted more and more towards biochemistry and biotechnology. Between the enzyme kinetics assays, protein purification, and metabolomics work in the Molecular Plant Physiology and Biotechnology lab, I barely recognise the ecological project we originally wrote.

I also keep thinking about how much my situation changed after papa’s minor stroke in early March 2025 and the start of his rehabilitation at AZ Sint-Jan in Bruges. The long trips to Ardennes field sites are no longer compatible with being able to accompany him to some of the neurology follow-up appointments and support my mother.

You and Tom have both been very supportive in practice, and I’m grateful for that. I would really appreciate your honest opinion on whether formally moving the project into the Biochemistry and Biotechnology doctoral programme is the best way to ensure the work is evaluated in an appropriate and supportive framework.

Best regards,
Lena

metadata.subject: Rough committee meeting + question about direction

artifacts.content:
WhatsApp chat – “Lab Rant” group

Lena: anyone awake or are you all buried under reviewer 2?

Sara: barely alive. What’s up?

Lena: just got another email about “aligning my outputs with community-level ecology journals” 🙃 after that advisory committee disaster on 21/10 in the Biology meeting room.

Jonas: omg, the one where they told you you were “deviating from the approved doctoral plan” for doing actual biochemistry? Still mad about that.

Lena: yes, that one. Meanwhile I spend 90% of my time in the Molecular Plant Physiology and Biotechnology lab doing protein work with Tom’s group.

Sara: tbh you’ve already switched in everything but paperwork.

Lena: exactly. And not to sound cynical, but it doesn’t help that Tom is way more influential on the Biochemistry & Biotechnology side. If I’m officially registered there, I’ll probably have better chances for internal stuff later – seminar slots, travel money, maybe even a postdoc bridge.

Jonas: that’s not cynical, that’s survival.

Lena: plus practical things: since papa’s stroke in early March and his rehab 2x/week at AZ Sint-Jan in Bruges, I can’t disappear to Ardennes field sites for days. Lab-based project in Ghent just makes sense.

Sara: also your brain? Didn’t your huisarts send you to CBT at UZ Gent because of anxiety about all this?

Lena: yep. Therapist keeps saying the split between “official Biology PhD” and “actual biochem work” is not helping my sleep.

Jonas: then switch. We’ll still invite you to ecology drinks 😜

Lena: deal. Now someone please remind me why I thought a PhD was a good idea.

Title: CBT session – UZ Gent (work anxiety)

Date: 2025-03-19
Time: 17:00–18:00
Location: UZ Gent, outpatient psychology
Attendees: Lena + psychologist

Notes:
- Ongoing cognitive behavioural therapy for work-related anxiety and insomnia (referred by huisarts in Nov 2024).
- Update on sleep: still waking up at 3–4 a.m. thinking about failed enzyme assays and that advisory committee warning in the Department of Biology meeting room on 21 Oct 2024 (“deviating from the approved doctoral plan”).
- Discuss how it feels to be officially in the Biology PhD (enrolled Oct 2024) while actually spending my days in the Molecular Plant Physiology and Biotechnology lab doing biochemistry and biotechnology.
- Bring up concrete stressors:
  • Papa’s minor stroke in early March 2025 → rehab 2x/week at AZ Sint-Jan Bruges + neurology follow-up appointments → need to stay based in Ghent, not in Ardennes field sites for days.
  • Financial strain from Feb–Jun 2025 field campaigns (~€1,200 upfront for trains + accommodation) → late rent (May & June) at Sint-Amandsbergstraat 42, bus 3 → temporary payment plan with landlord.
- Goal for session: map how much of the anxiety is from general perfectionism vs. structural mismatch between official doctoral programme (Biology) and actual project (biochemistry & biotechnology).
- Homework: write pros/cons list of formally switching to Doctor of Philosophy in Biochemistry and Biotechnology; track sleep for 2 weeks.

Subject: Money stress + Ardennes trips

Hi mama,

Just finished feeding Pixel (he pretends he’s starving every two hours) and wanted to answer your message from yesterday.

You asked why I sounded so tired on the phone. Honestly, a big part of it is the money stress from the Ardennes fieldwork on top of everything with papa. Between February and June 2025 I’ve spent over €1,200 on extra train tickets and cheap rooms for those multi-day field campaigns. The reimbursements from the department are taking ages, so in May and June I didn’t have enough in my account when the rent for Sint-Amandsbergstraat 42, bus 3 was due. I had to email the landlord and set up a temporary payment plan. It’s sorted now, but it was really embarrassing.

It doesn’t help that I’m hardly ever in Bruges when papa has his rehab sessions twice a week at AZ Sint-Jan or the neurology follow-up appointments. I hate the feeling that I’m counting plant communities in the Ardennes while you’re doing everything alone there.

That’s why I’m so relieved that the project with Prof. Dr. Tom Janssens is almost entirely lab-based in Ghent, in the Molecular Plant Physiology and Biotechnology lab. His project budget covers most of the experimental costs directly, so I’m not constantly fronting money. If I can fully move into that biochemistry-focused trajectory, it should be much more stable for the next 3–4 years.

I’ll come on Sunday like we said. Maybe we can walk with papa a bit after his exercises if he’s not too tired.

Love,
Lena
